_doc: Skeptic POV taxonomy. One file per POV camp to minimize merge conflicts.

nodes.label: Protect Human Jobs from AI
nodes.description: Companies are using AI to replace workers and cut costs, which could leave many people without a job. We need laws that require companies to retrain employees for new roles or provide financial support during the transition. It's like putting speed bumps in a neighborhood to slow down cars. We're not banning the technology, just making sure the change happens at a safe speed for everyone.

nodes.label: Make AI Systems Truly Fair
nodes.description: AI systems often learn from biased data, which means they can make unfair decisions about things like loans or jobs, especially for minority groups. This goal is about forcing companies to check their AI for bias and fix it. Think of it like a referee in a game who makes sure the rules are applied equally to every player. Technology should make society more just, not just automate old prejudices.

nodes.label: Stop AI from Spying On Us
nodes.description: AI gives companies and governments powerful tools to track what we do, say, and buy without our permission. We need to create strong rules that protect our personal information from being collected and used against us. It’s like building a digital fence around our private lives. Without this privacy, our freedom to speak and act without being constantly watched is at risk.

nodes.label: The Proof of AI's Unfairness
nodes.description: This is the hard evidence showing how AI systems are making biased decisions in the real world. For example, some AI used for job hiring has been shown to favor men over women, while some facial recognition systems are less accurate for people of color. This data proves that AI isn't neutral. It often learns and amplifies human prejudices, causing real harm to specific groups of people.

nodes.label: Proof That AI Is Taking Jobs
nodes.description: This data gathers concrete numbers and reports on jobs that are being replaced by AI right now. We're not talking about factory robots, but AI doing office work like writing reports, analyzing documents, and handling customer service. This information shows the real-world pressure AI is putting on people's careers today. It helps us understand the true impact of this technology on the workforce.

nodes.label: Don't Fall for Sci-Fi Hype
nodes.description: Some people talk a lot about hypothetical dangers, like a future evil super-AI taking over the world. This is often a distraction tactic. It shifts attention away from the real problems AI is causing right now, like job losses and biased decisions. It's like a magician making you look at one hand while the other one does the trick, letting companies off the hook for current harms.

nodes.label: Solve Real AI Problems Now
nodes.description: We should focus on making rules that fix the AI problems we can actually see and measure. This means tackling issues like biased loan applications or AI systems that violate our privacy. We shouldn't waste time and resources making policies for made-up, futuristic dangers. It’s about being a practical mechanic who fixes the rattling engine in front of them, not one who worries about the car spontaneously flying away.

nodes.label: Require Independent AI Safety Checks
nodes.description: Before a new drug is sold, it has to be tested by independent experts to prove it's safe. The same rule should apply to powerful AI systems, especially those making decisions about our health, money, or freedom. Companies shouldn't be allowed to just say "trust us, it works." Having a separate, unbiased group test these systems for fairness and safety is the only way to protect the public.

nodes.label: AI Is Poisoning Scientific Data
nodes.description: Scientists are starting to use AI to help analyze their research data because it's fast. The problem is that the AI has its own built-in biases, and when it labels information, those biases get mixed into the official data. This makes the data look like objective human findings when it's not. It’s like using a photocopier that adds a tiny smudge to every copy, which quietly ruins the trustworthiness of scientific knowledge over time.

nodes.label: Use Tech for People, Not Profit
nodes.description: Huge companies have built amazing AI and automation systems to make money. Instead of letting this technology only benefit the rich, we should take control of it and use it for everyone. Imagine if automated systems were used to provide food, housing, and goods for all, freeing us from jobs we don't want to do. This isn't about destroying technology, but redirecting its power to build a better, more equal society.

nodes.label: AI Models Can Leak Your Data
nodes.description: An AI model is like a sponge that soaks up all the data it's trained on. Even after it's 'trained,' you can squeeze that sponge in certain ways to get some of the original data back out. This means private information could be reconstructed just by interacting with the AI. It's not about someone hacking a server; the AI's own design is the security flaw.

nodes.label: Tech Workers Can Remake Society
nodes.description: In the past, factory workers had the power to shut down production. Today, the people who design software and manage data networks have a similar power because they build the digital 'machinery' that runs our world. This means tech and creative workers could decide to repurpose these powerful tools for everyone's benefit, not just for big companies.

nodes.label: AI Deepfakes Aren't That Convincing
nodes.description: Despite all the hype about AI-generated 'deepfakes,' there's little proof they've actually changed election results or caused major social problems. People are still better at spotting fakes than we think, and old-fashioned, human-led propaganda is far more effective. The fear of a world overrun by AI lies is not supported by the facts we have today.

nodes.label: Everyone Gets a Share of AI
nodes.description: As AI and robots start doing more jobs, fewer people will earn money from traditional work. One solution is to give every citizen a small ownership stake in the technology itself, like owning stock in a company. This way, as these systems create wealth, everyone gets a payout, or a 'dividend.' It's about shifting our economy from being based on what you do to being based on what you own.

nodes.label: Value What Really Matters
nodes.description: Our society measures value mostly in dollars, based on work or products. This idea suggests using technology to create new systems that reward other things, like contributing to open-source software or sharing knowledge. Imagine a digital currency that you earn by helping your community or creating public art. It's about using tech to build an economy that values social cooperation, not just financial profit.

nodes.label: Stop a Few Companies From Owning AI
nodes.description: Right now, only a handful of giant companies have the massive computer power and data needed to build the most powerful AI. This creates a dangerous monopoly, giving them too much economic and political control. The goal is to break this up by supporting open-source AI models and building public computing resources, like a public library for AI. This ensures the benefits of AI are shared by everyone, not just a small tech elite.

nodes.label: Humans Become AI Managers
nodes.description: AI won't just replace jobs; it will change them. Instead of doing repetitive tasks, more people will work on supervising the AI systems that do those tasks. Think of it like a pilot who manages a plane's autopilot instead of flying manually all the time. New roles are emerging where the job is to guide, check, and fix the work of automated systems.

nodes.label: Build Safety Fences Around AI
nodes.description: Instead of trying to create a perfectly safe AI, it's more practical to build safety measures around how it's used. Think of a car: we add seatbelts, create traffic laws, and build guardrails on roads. Similarly, we can build filters, legal rules, and defensive software to prevent AI from being used for specific harmful things, without limiting the core technology itself.

nodes.label: Plan For AI Messes
nodes.description: Complex technology will always have bugs and unexpected failures. Instead of trying to prevent every possible AI risk, we should focus on getting good at recovering when things go wrong. This is like having firefighters and paramedics ready for emergencies, rather than trying to ban everything that could possibly start a fire. It means creating systems to quickly detect errors, fix them, and help those who are affected.

nodes.label: AI Is Not An Alien Invasion
nodes.description: We should think of AI like other major inventions, such as the printing press or electricity. It's a powerful tool that will change society, but it will do so gradually and be shaped by our existing laws and institutions. The idea that AI is a totally unique, uncontrollable force is overblown. We should focus on fixing its real-world problems step-by-step, just like we've done with every other technology.

nodes.label: Demand High-Quality AI Ingredients
nodes.description: Think of training an AI like making a healthy meal; you wouldn't just grab random junk off the street to cook with. This approach insists that AI creators must use well-documented, verified information instead of just scraping the entire messy internet. This prevents the AI from learning and repeating the biases and nonsense found online, making it more reliable and fair.

nodes.label: AI's Hidden Environmental Price Tag
nodes.description: Training huge AI models uses massive amounts of energy and water, creating a large carbon footprint. This data tracks the real-world environmental damage and the immense financial cost of building AI. Often, big tech companies reap the rewards while poorer communities deal with the environmental fallout, making it impossible for smaller groups to compete.

nodes.label: AI Is A Pattern-Matching Parrot
nodes.description: AI models are not thinking beings; they are incredibly advanced prediction machines. Imagine a student who memorized every book in the library but doesn't understand what the words actually mean. The AI is just very good at guessing the next word in a sentence based on patterns, which is why we shouldn't treat it like a person and why it sometimes makes up nonsensical 'facts'.

nodes.label: Making Our Brains Lazy with AI
nodes.description: If you use a calculator for every simple math problem, you might forget how to do it in your head. Similarly, relying too much on AI for writing or research can weaken our own critical thinking and memory skills. We can also fall into the trap of automatically trusting an AI's answer without checking if it's actually correct.

nodes.label: AI Used for Digital Harassment
nodes.description: Generative AI is being used to create fake explicit photos and videos of people, particularly women and girls, without their permission. These deepfakes are a powerful and dangerous tool for bullying, harassment, and abuse. This is not a future problem; it is a real and immediate harm happening right now.

nodes.label: Separating Real Problems From Sci-Fi
nodes.description: This approach sorts AI risks into two buckets: solvable puzzles and unpredictable messes. A puzzle is a risk we can figure out with more data, like tracking an asteroid, while a mess is just chaotic, like a stock market crash. We should focus on fixing the known, messy problems AI causes today, not get distracted by far-fetched sci-fi scenarios.

nodes.label: Sharing AI Profits With Everyone
nodes.description: As AI becomes more powerful, it could make companies incredibly wealthy while replacing many human jobs. This idea proposes that a portion of the massive profits from AI should be collected and shared with everyone in the world. It would work like a regular payment to help support people whose jobs might be disrupted by new technology.

nodes.label: Make AI Cite Its Sources
nodes.description: AI models learn by studying huge amounts of data, including art, music, and writing created by people. This rule would require AI companies to publish a list of all the copyrighted works they used for training. It's like a bibliography for an AI, ensuring artists and writers know if their work was used and can protect their rights.
